title: Strings: Arrays of Characters
subtitle: Text is more structured than you think

sections.heading: Strings are character arrays
sections.body: A **string** is just a sequence of characters. You can index into it like an array — `name[0]` gives the first letter. The difference: strings are **immutable**. You can't change a character in place; you build a new string.

sections.title: Beads on a string
sections.body: Imagine a necklace of letter beads. You can read each bead, count them, find a specific one. But you can't swap a bead without breaking the necklace and remaking it. That's how strings work in JavaScript.

sections.code:
const word = "hello";

console.log(word.length);        // 5
console.log(word[0]);            // "h"
console.log(word.toUpperCase()); // "HELLO" (new string)
console.log(word);               // "hello" (original unchanged)

console.log(word.includes("ell")); // true
console.log(word.split(""));       // ["h","e","l","l","o"]

sections.prompt: Write `isPalindrome(s)` that returns true if s reads the same forwards and backwards.
hints: split('') turns a string into an array of characters.
join('') turns an array back into a string.

questions.question: Which is TRUE about JavaScript strings?
options: You can change individual characters with str[0] = 'x'
Strings are immutable — methods return new strings
questions.explanation: Strings can't be modified in place. Methods like toUpperCase() return a new string.
